stories.headline: Arch Manning's Moment Has Finally Arrived
stories.summary: After two seasons learning behind Quinn Ewers, Arch Manning steps into 2026 as the unquestioned face of Texas football. Every indicator — arm talent, command of the offense, leadership — points to a special year.
stories.body:
There's a version of the Arch Manning story that gets told as a burden — the weight of a last name, the impossible expectations, the years of waiting while lesser quarterbacks took his reps. That version misses the point entirely.

Manning didn't just survive those two years. He studied. He absorbed everything Ewers did wrong and right. He watched how defenses adjusted game to game, how Sarkisian's system bends to what the quarterback does best. He went through two springs, two fall camps, and countless hours in the film room before he ever took a meaningful snap under pressure.

The 2025 numbers tell part of the story: 3,472 passing yards, 29 touchdowns, 8 interceptions, 68.4% completion percentage. Good numbers. Not great ones. Manning himself would tell you the same — he made too many decisions on guesswork, held the ball too long against LSU's second-half pressure, missed windows on crossing routes that he now diagnoses in his sleep.

But that's the 2025 version. The 2026 version has a full offseason of being the unquestioned starter, has Cam Coleman installed at WR1 giving him a true downfield weapon, and has a defense built to win games that don't need to be track meets.

Sarkisian has been clear: the offense is going to ask Manning to be more decisive, not more creative. Less hero ball. More execution. For a quarterback with this much natural talent, that's the most dangerous combination in college football.

NIL valuations at $4.2M mean Manning is operating in a different commercial universe than any college quarterback before him. That could be noise. With this group, it feels like signal.

Hook 'em.
takeaways: Manning posted 3,472 yards and 29 TDs in 2025 — his first full season as starter
Cam Coleman's arrival gives him a genuine WR1 target for the first time
Sarkisian's system is built around decisiveness — Manning's growth there is the key variable

stories.headline: The Cam Coleman Effect: What Changes About Everything
stories.summary: Cam Coleman was the most electrifying skill player Auburn landed in a decade. He's in Austin now. Here's exactly what his presence does to Sarkisian's offense — and why defensive coordinators in the SEC have no clean answer.
stories.body:
When Auburn fans describe Cam Coleman, they use words like 'unfair.' As in: it's unfair that a college cornerback has to deal with a receiver who runs 4.3 with hands that don't drop anything and a release that makes press coverage a mistake.

Now he belongs to Texas.

The immediate impact on the offense is structural. Ryan Wingo is a legitimate WR1 — he proved that last year with 38 catches and 624 yards on limited targets. But Wingo worked best when defenses couldn't key on him. With Coleman on the field, that becomes a permanent problem for opposing coordinators.

You cannot bracket both. You cannot cover both in man coverage without leaving someone else unaccounted for. And Texas still has Jermaine Bishop as a third option, tight ends who can stress linebackers, and Raleek Brown out of the backfield.

This is what Sarkisian's offense looks like at full strength. Not the 2022 version, not the 2024 version. This version, with a quarterback who's been in the system three years and a WR room that creates genuinely unsolvable matchup problems.

Coleman ran the full route tree in spring practice. By all accounts he and Manning already have timing down on deep comeback routes — the pattern that was most inconsistently thrown by Texas quarterbacks last season. That matters.
takeaways: Coleman and Wingo give Texas two legitimate WR1-caliber receivers for the first time under Sarkisian
Defenses cannot bracket cover both — someone will be one-on-one all game
His chemistry with Manning on deep routes was noted heavily in spring reports

stories.headline: The Case for Colin Simmons as the Best Pass Rusher in College Football
stories.summary: Eleven sacks. Seventeen TFLs. Six forced fumbles. Simmons posted those numbers as a sophomore. The Bednarik conversation isn't hype — it's the only logical conclusion when you watch the tape.
stories.body:
The first thing that stands out on film is not the athleticism. You expect that. What you don't expect from a sophomore pass rusher is the patience.

Simmons doesn't attack blocks. He reads them. He feels where the tackle wants to take him and then he counters — inside swim if the tackle oversets, speed-to-power if they give ground early, a dip-and-bend on the outside when the corner is clean.

Eleven sacks doesn't capture it. Five of those came in the fourth quarter of close games, when offensive coordinators were calling protection-heavy dropbacks and still couldn't keep him out of the backfield. That's the part that scouts notice.

At 6'3", 245 pounds, Simmons projects as a true 4-3 defensive end at the next level — the kind who can play the run on early downs and still be a three-down nightmare. Texas uses him differently, in a 4-2-5 look that sometimes puts him as a stand-up edge, sometimes as a hand-in-the-dirt end, sometimes in coverage to show formation diversity.

He handles all of it.

The Bednarik Award goes to the best defensive player in college football. In 2026, the question isn't whether Simmons is on the list. The question is whether anyone else has a real argument.
takeaways: Simmons posted 11 sacks and 17 TFLs as a sophomore — production that projects to Bednarik-level
His patience and counter-move arsenal distinguish him from pure athletic pass rushers
NFL scouts view him as a Day 1 pick with three-down versatility

stories.headline: How the Transfer Portal Rebuilt Texas — And What It Cost
stories.summary: Eleven incoming transfers. Eight departures including two first-round picks. The math adds up to more than bodies — it reflects a philosophy shift in how Sarkisian is building this program.
stories.body:
The 2026 Texas portal class isn't the biggest in the country. It's the most targeted.

Sarkisian didn't chase volume. He identified three specific needs — a WR1 with immediate-starter upside, a veteran corner who could replace NFL talent, and an experienced running back to complement the depth already on the roster — and he attacked each of them surgically.

Cam Coleman (WR, Auburn), Derek Williams Jr. (CB, Penn State), and Raleek Brown (RB, Oklahoma State) are all projected immediate starters. None of them came cheap in the NIL market. All three were among the top transfers available at their positions.

The departures are harder. Quinn Ewers going to the NFL was always the plan. Kelvin Banks Jr. and Anthony Hill Jr. leaving as first-round picks is program success — you want your players to get paid. But losing that much proven production at once requires the portal to work, and it did.

The net gain is real. Texas is deeper, older at the positions that matter most, and has added players who've won in big games at big programs. That's not a given when you run the portal hard.
takeaways: Texas targeted exactly three portal needs and landed all three top targets
Coleman, Williams Jr., and Brown are all projected immediate starters
The departures of Ewers, Banks, and Hill represent program success, not loss

stories.headline: 48–20, Two CFP Trips, Zero Titles: An Honest Sark Era Assessment
stories.summary: The numbers say one thing. The program's trajectory says another. We measure Sarkisian's Texas tenure against what was promised, what was delivered, and what 2026 has to prove.
stories.body:
Forty-eight wins. Twenty losses. Two College Football Playoff appearances. No championships.

Depending on how you frame it, Steve Sarkisian has either exceeded expectations or underperformed them. Both reads are defensible. Neither is complete.

The realistic expectation when Sarkisian was hired in 2021 was program stabilization followed by contention. He delivered both, faster than most programs can manage a turnaround. The 2021 season was ugly — 5-7, no bowl, a learning curve that looked steeper than anyone wanted to admit. But from 2022 onward, the trajectory has been consistently upward.

What hasn't arrived is a championship. Two CFP appearances that ended in losses before the final. A 2023 Big 12 title run that put Texas back on the national map but couldn't close in the semifinal. A 2025 SEC debut that showed the program belongs but didn't show it can win the whole thing.

That's the unfinished business. Sarkisian's staff is good. His recruiting is elite. His offense is creative. But there's a ceiling question that only a deep playoff run answers.

2026 is the year that either answers it or extends the conversation indefinitely.
takeaways: Sarkisian's 48-20 record is legitimate program progress after years of instability
Two CFP trips without a title is the honest summary of the era so far
2026 is the defining season — the roster talent leaves no excuses

stories.headline: 2027 Priority Board: The Targets That Define the Next Class
stories.summary: The 2026 class is signed. Now the staff has shifted focus entirely to 2027. Here's what the priority board looks like, which positions are urgent, and where Texas stands with the targets that matter most.
stories.body:
The 2026 class closed at #8 nationally. The staff isn't satisfied with that. Internally, the benchmark is top 5 every cycle. That means the 2027 class needs to outperform, and the work is already well underway.

The most urgent need on the board is EDGE. Texas graduates significant production from the position after 2026 — even with Simmons returning for his junior year, the depth behind him is thin. The staff is targeting two EDGE rushers in the 2027 class with the priority to get at least one committed before the season starts.

Wide receiver is the second priority. Coleman and Wingo are both entering their final seasons of eligibility. The staff needs a high-ceiling WR prospect in 2027 to ensure the position doesn't drop off after 2028.

At quarterback, there's an interesting situation developing. Dia Bell comes in as the 2026 QB commit with legitimate upside, but the staff is still evaluating whether to take a second quarterback in 2027 depending on how the depth chart shakes out.

The recruiting calendar runs through summer official visits, fall junior days, and the December early signing period. Texas is currently the leader for three prospects rated inside the top 30 nationally. That's a good place to be in June.
takeaways: EDGE and WR are the two highest-priority positions for the 2027 class
Texas is the leader for three top-30 prospects nationally heading into summer
The staff's internal benchmark is top-5 nationally — 2026's #8 finish was below standard
